items.title: Move Gaussian splatting autograd and pipeline logic from C++ to Python
items.body:
## Summary

- Port all C++ autograd functions (projection, rasterization, SH evaluation) to pure Python `torch.autograd.Function` implementations in a new `_gaussian_autograd.py` module, making the differentiable pipeline easier to extend and debug.
- Eliminate the C++ `GaussianSplat3d` class entirely: the Python `GaussianSplat3d` class now directly manages `torch.Tensor` attributes instead of delegating to a C++ `_impl` object.
- Rewrite `deduplicatePixels` as a pure Python function using PyTorch ops, removing its C++ binding and implementation.
- Refactor PLY I/O to return a flat tuple of tensors (no intermediate struct), and update the viewer to accept individual tensors directly instead of a C++ object.
- Remove ~7,600 lines of dead C++ code (`GaussianSplat3d.{h,cpp}`, `detail/autograd/*`, `DeduplicatePixelsTest.cpp`, `GaussianSplat3dCameraApiTest.cpp`).
- Simplify pybind bindings to only expose low-level dispatch functions; all autograd wiring now lives in Python.

## Test plan

- [x] All 135 Python unit tests pass (`cd tests && pytest unit/test_gaussian_splat_3d.py -v`)
- [x] All 21 C++ gtests pass (`ctest --test-dir build/.../src/tests`)
- [x] CI passes (codestyle, DCO, full test matrix)

items.title: [bugfix] Rewrite transfer metrics from C++ to Python side
items.body:
Previously, transfer metrics (ops_total, bytes_total) were recorded in the C++ layer (transfer.cu, transfer_ssd.cpp, gds_manager.cpp) via MetricsManager macros. Although C++ has its own Prometheus exporter (port 8081), the actual data transfer code runs in forked worker subprocesses, which do not initialize the C++ Prometheus metrics server. As a result, these transfer metrics were never collected at all.

Key changes:
- Rewrite transfer metrics collection entirely on the Python side with a post-completion approach, recording metrics in the main process after transfer operations complete
- Add `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to Python collector for tracking data transfer throughput
- Remove `flexkv_cpp_transfer_ops_total` and `flexkv_cpp_transfer_bytes_total` counters from C++ MetricsManager

Changes include:
- Extended `CompletedOp` in `flexkv/common/transfer.py` with `transfer_type`, `num_blocks`, and `num_bytes` fields
- Compute transfer bytes in `TransferEngine` (`transfer_engine.py`) when an op finishes
- Record all three counters (ops_total, blocks_total, bytes_total) via `record_transfer_completed()` in `KVTaskManager._update_tasks()` (`kvtask.py`), ensuring metrics are collected in the main process
- Added `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to `flexkv/metrics/collector.py`
- Removed old `_record_transfer_ops()` from `cache_engine.py`
- Removed C++ transfer metric recording from `transfer.cu`, `transfer_ssd.cpp`, and `gds_manager.cpp`
- Updated Grafana dashboard (`flexkv-demo.json`): added Transfer Throughput panel, replaced C++ Transfer Throughput with Cache Blocks Rate panel
- Updated monitoring documentation at `docs/monitoring/README_en.md` (English) and `docs/monitoring/README_zh.md` (Chinese)

items.title: Move sensor metadata from C++ to Python schema (fixes compilation with ESPHome 2026.3)
items.body:
ESPHome 2026.3 removed undocumented `EntityBase` runtime setters such as `set_unit_of_measurement()` and `set_device_class()`. This component still used those setters in several custom sensor constructors, which caused the build failure.

This PR migrates those sensor metadata fields to the Python schema/codegen layer and removes the obsolete C++ setter calls.

Affected sensors:
- `CompressorFrequencySensor` (Hz, frequency)
- `InputPowerSensor` (W, power)
- `kWhSensor` (kWh, energy)
- `RuntimeHoursSensor` (h, duration)
- `OutsideAirTemperatureSensor` (°C, temperature)

## Notes

This follows the migration path expected by ESPHome 2026.3: entity metadata is now provided through schema/codegen and packed into `configure_entity_()` instead of being set from runtime C++ constructors.

⚠️ **This is not a cosmetic change.** The sensor metadata path changes entirely: from C++ runtime to Python codegen. Please verify carefully:

- [x] All five sensors appear correctly in Home Assistant with proper unit and device_class (not "unknown" or blank)
- [x] Historical data / long-term statistics are not disrupted (same entity IDs, same units; HA should see continuity)
- [x] YAML overrides still work (e.g. setting a custom `unit_of_measurement` on any of these sensors)
- [x] No regressions on compressor_frequency reporting during active heating/cooling cycles
- [x] kWh sensor still uses `state_class: total_increasing` (critical for energy dashboard)

It would also be good to test it with older ESPHome versions to confirm backward compatibility. I would expect it to remain compatible, but it’s still worth verifying explicitly.

items.title: Berry solidification made possible with Python runtime
items.body:
## Description:

The Berry runtime was ported from `C` to `Python` which allows to move solidification stage into the toolchain. The goal is eventually to introduce new features like conditional compilation (smaller firmwares) and text localization.

The porting took less than a week using Kiro and Claude Opus 4.6. It generates a byte-to-byte compatible solidification source code.

items.title: Migrate from Unreal Engine to Python show control system
items.body:
## Summary

This is a major architectural refactor that migrates the CommunityGarden show control system from Unreal Engine 5 (C++) to Python 3.11+. The core functionality remains the same—detecting people via depth camera and driving servo motors—but the implementation is now lightweight, cross-platform, and easier to iterate on.

## Key Changes

- **Language & Framework:** Replaced UE5 C++ codebase with Python modules (`flower_beds.py`, `blob_tracker.py`, `blob_stabilizer.py`, `camera.py`, `transforms.py`, `flower_controller.py`, `visualizer.py`)
- **Vision Pipeline:** Replaced `IIVision` plugin with custom numpy/scipy blob detection (background subtraction + connected-component analysis + 3D unprojection)
- **Blob Tracking:** Implemented greedy nearest-neighbour matching with EMA smoothing in `BlobStabilizer` for cross-frame stability
- **Configuration:** Replaced INI-based `UDeveloperSettings` with JSON config file (`settings.json`) for cameras, modules, clusters, and controllers
- **Networking:** Maintained OSC/UDP communication to Arduino; now via `python-osc` library
- **Visualization:** Added FastAPI WebSocket server (`visualizer.py`) for live top-down debug view in browser
- **Documentation:** Updated CLAUDE.md with Python conventions, module responsibilities, architecture patterns, and CLI usage

## Implementation Details

- **Coordinate System:** Preserved UE-style world coordinates (X=forward, Y=right, Z=up, cm) with helper functions in `transforms.py` for camera↔world conversion
- **Calibration:** `BlobTracker` collects configurable number of frames to build per-pixel median background before detection begins
- **Stabilization:** `BlobStabilizer` suppresses jitter with configurable EMA alpha and enforces minimum confirmation frames before tracks are emitted
- **Main Loop:** `main.py` orchestrates frame capture → detection → stabilization → cluster assignment → OSC output → visualization broadcast
- **Mock Mode:** `--mock` flag enables testing without hardware; `--no-osc` disables Arduino output for dry runs
- **Headless Operation:** `--no-visualizer` flag allows deployment without WebSocket server overhead

All motor IDs, network addresses, and positions remain configurable in `settings.json`; no hardcoded values.

fix(ci): repair router index summary step quoting
items.body:
## Summary

CI run [24215707813](https://github.com/Mathews-Tom/armory/actions/runs/24215707813/job/70695596474) failed on the \`Summary\` step of \`.github/workflows/router-index.yml\` with:

\`\`\`
File \"<string>\", line 5
    print(f\"- Built at: {meta[\\\"built_at\\\"]}\")
                               ^
SyntaxError: unexpected character after line continuation character
\`\`\`

### Root cause

The step invoked \`python -c '...'\` inside a single-quoted shell string containing \`meta[\\\"built_at\\\"]\`. Bash passed the backslashes through verbatim, so Python parsed \`\\\"\` as a source-level line-continuation token followed by a bare quote. PEP 701 (Python 3.12) lifted the restriction on backslashes inside f-string *expressions*, but \`\\\"\` is still a line-continuation token at the tokenizer level — relaxing the f-string rules does not rescue this snippet.

### Fix

- Switch from \`python -c '...'\` to a \`python - <<'PY' ... PY\` heredoc so the Python source is passed literally and no shell escaping is involved.
- Use single-quoted dict key access inside the f-strings (\`meta['built_at']\` instead of \`meta[\\\"built_at\\\"]\`).

### Verification

Reproduced the \`run:\` block in a local bash shell against a synthetic \`dist/router_index.json\` (\`bash -e <<'SCRIPT' ... SCRIPT\`) and confirmed the heredoc executes cleanly and writes the expected lines to \`\$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY\`:

\`\`\`
### Router Index Rebuild
- Built at: 2026-04-09T00:00:00Z
- Source entries: 10
- Unique signatures: 8
- Unique packages: 4
- Min samples: 3
\`\`\`

## Test plan

- [ ] Re-run the \`Router Index Rebuild\` workflow on this branch and confirm the \`Summary\` step exits 0.
- [ ] Verify the GitHub Actions step summary panel shows the five metadata lines.

items.title: Migrate AICore kernel compilation from C++ to Python and simplify DeviceRunner API
items.body:
This commit fundamentally restructures how AICore kernels are compiled and loaded, moving kernel compilation responsibility from C++ to Python and simplifying the device runtime API.

Key changes:

**Kernel Compilation Migration:**
- Extract kernel compilation from C++ (kernel_compiler.cpp/h) to Python
- Add PTOCompiler class for Python-side kernel compilation using ccec
- Add elf_parser.py for extracting .text sections from ELF object files
- Remove binary_loader.cpp/h and kernel_compiler.cpp/h (replaced by Python)
- Kernels now compiled and registered at Python layer before runtime execution

**DeviceRunner API Simplification:**
- Replace DeviceRunner Init/Run/Finalize with set_device() and launch_graph()
- Remove pto_isa_root parameter (no longer needed after moving compilation to Python)
- Add RegisterKernel() API for kernel binary registration
- DeviceRunner now auto-finalizes via destructor (no manual Finalize needed)
- Device initialization split into set_device() (early) and lazy binary loading (in launch_graph)

**Runtime API Changes:**
- Change Runtime allocation from C++ new/delete to placement new pattern
- Add GetGraphSize() for user-side buffer allocation
- Rename ValidateGraph() to FinalizeGraph() to better reflect responsibility
- Remove function_cache.h (kernel lookup now uses direct registration)

**AICore Executor Abstraction:**
- Extract aicore_executor.h with core type and device args abstraction
- Add DeviceArgs struct to replace uint64_t kernel parameters
- Refactor kernel.cpp to use new executor pattern

**Documentation and Testing:**
- Update example/main.py to demonstrate new Python-first workflow
- Simplify README testing instructions (use ./ci.sh)
- Add ci.sh for streamlined testing

Net result: -270 lines, cleaner separation of concerns, Python has full control over kernel compilation and registration lifecycle.

items.title: Fix: replace `sed c\` with Python script for README section updates
items.body:
`Update Project Statistics` workflow has been failing consistently with `sed: -e expression #1, char 160: unknown command: '-'`. The `sed c\` (change) command chokes when `$PROJECTS_LIST` expands to markdown list items (`- **[repo]...**`) — the leading `-` on a continuation line is misinterpreted as a sed command flag.

## Changes

- **`.github/scripts/update_readme.py`** — new Python script that replaces both broken `sed` operations:
  - Replaces the `### 主要项目` … `### 项目特点` block with the current project list
  - Replaces the `### 项目统计` table (header + all data rows) with fresh stats
  - Uses `re.sub()` with explicit patterns, safely handling any markdown content

- **`.github/workflows/update-stats.yml`** — replaces three fragile `sed` commands (two `c\` range-replacements + one `/r` file-insert) with:
  ```bash
  printf '%b' "$PROJECTS_LIST" > /tmp/projects_list.txt
  python3 .github/scripts/update_readme.py /tmp/projects_list.txt stats_table.md profile/README.md
  ```
  `printf '%b'` is used instead of `echo -e` to correctly expand `\n` escape sequences when writing to the temp file.

items.title: Support zipapp mode
items.body:
Fix #54 to support zipapp
This is also the first time to adopt claude code to write to code.
Push the .claude later


##  Summary

  - Add support for building encrypted .pye modules into .pyz zip archives via pyecli build-zip command
  - Add PyeZipImporter — a PEP-451 finder/loader that imports .pye modules from inside zip files on sys.path
  - The pyconcrete executable now detects .pyz files and executes __main__.pye from the archive
  - Delegate .pye/.pyz execution logic from C to Python (run_pye() / run_pyz()), simplifying the C layer significantly

##  Key Changes

  New: pyecli build-zip command
  - Compiles .py files to .pye and packs them into a .pyz/.zip archive
  - Supports --main pkg.mod:fn entry point generation (similar to python -m zipapp)
  - Supports --ext, --ignore-file-list, and --source/--output options

  New: PyeZipImporter (zip import hook)
  - Registers on sys.path_hooks to transparently find and decrypt .pye modules inside zip archives
  - Thread-safe directory cache with negative cache for non-zip paths
  - Falls back to standard zipimport for non-.pye entries

  Refactored: C executable (pyconcrete_exe.c)
  - Removed C-level execPycContent() and file-reading logic (~80 lines deleted)
  - runFile() and new runPyzFile() now delegate to Python-side pyconcrete.run_pye() / pyconcrete.run_pyz()
  - Added isZipFile() to detect zip magic bytes and route execution accordingly

##  Test Plan

  - tests/test_exe_pyz.py — end-to-end tests for .pyz execution via the pyconcrete executable
  - tests/test_zip_import.py — unit tests for PyeZipImporter (module import, package import, nested imports from zip)
  - Existing tests/test_exe.py updated for the refactored execution flow

- Type hints on all functions

items.title: Convert repository from JavaScript/Perl/C to Python
items.body:
Converts four RPG generators (name, random text, dungeon, world map) from JavaScript, Perl, and C to Python for improved maintainability and cross-platform compatibility.

## Conversions

- **Name Generator** (JavaScript → Python): Markov chain implementation with chain caching and weighted token selection
- **Random Generator** (JavaScript → Python): Template expansion with recursive token replacement and weighted table selection
- **Dungeon Generator** (Perl → Python, 1599→920 lines): Room placement, corridor generation, door types, stair placement. Replaces Perl GD with Pillow
- **World Generator** (C → Python, 1104→360 lines): Fractal terrain via fault line algorithm, histogram-based sea level, flood-fill ice caps. Replaces custom GIF encoding with Pillow

## Usage

```python
# Name generation
from namegenerator.name_generator import generate_name, name_list
import namegenerator.egyptian_set as egyptian_set
egyptian_set.load_egyptian_set()
names = name_list('egyptian', 10)

# Dungeon generation
from dungeon.dungeon import DungeonGenerator
dungeon = DungeonGenerator(seed=12345, n_rows=39, n_cols=39)
dungeon.generate().save_image('dungeon.gif')

# World generation
from worldgen.worldgen import WorldGenerator
world = WorldGenerator(width=320, height=160, num_faults=1000)
world.generate().save_image('world.gif')
```

- Added `requirements.txt` (Pillow>=10.0.0)

items.title: Translate Grey Wolf Optimizer from C# to Python with unified interface
items.body:
Implements GWO algorithm in Python to match the existing BOA and PSO implementations, translating from the C# version while standardizing the interface.

## Implementation

- **GWO class**: Inherits from `IOptimizationAlgorithm` with properties `Name`, `XBest`, `FBest`, `EvalCount`
- **Constructor signature**: `GWO(obj_func, dim, lb, ub, pop_size, max_iter, a_coeff=2.0, c_coeff=2.0)`
  - Unified `pop_size` parameter (C# used `wolves`)
  - Updated default coefficients: `a_coeff=2.0` (was 1.0), `c_coeff=2.0` (was 3.0)
- **Solve() method**: Merges C# `Optimize()` into `Solve()` for consistency
- **Fitness evaluation**: Initial population + after each position update (matches C# pattern)
  - `EvalCount = pop_size + pop_size * max_iter`

## Algorithm Details

Three leader wolves (alpha, beta, delta) guide the pack:
```python
# Position update based on weighted average of leaders
X1 = alpha_pos[j] - A1 * abs(C1 * alpha_pos[j] - positions[i, j])
X2 = beta_pos[j] - A2 * abs(C2 * beta_pos[j] - positions[i, j])
X3 = delta_pos[j] - A3 * abs(C3 * delta_pos[j] - positions[i, j])
positions[i, j] = (X1 + X2 + X3) / 3.0
```

Parameter `a` decreases linearly from `a_coeff` to 0 over iterations. Boundary violations use reflection with 10% of range.

## Test Framework

- Five benchmark functions: Rastrigin, Rosenbrock, Sphere, Beale, Bukin N.6
- `run_tests()` function with statistics (mean, std, best/worst over n runs)
- `iteration_history` tracks convergence

## Fixes

- C# `Name` property had infinite recursion (`get { return Name; }`), now correctly implemented
- Consistent snake_case naming throughout

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Cel
Przetłumaczyć algorytm GWO (Grey Wolf Optimizer) z C# na Python i dostosować do wspólnego interfejsu `IOptimizationAlgorithm`, aby był zgodny z algorytmami BOA i PSO.

## Wymagania

### 1. Utworzyć nowy plik `gwo.py`

### 2. Zaimplementować klasę GWO zgodną z interfejsem

Kod powinien używać tego samego interfejsu `IOptimizationAlgorithm` co BOA:

```python
import numpy as np
import time
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class IOptimizationAlgorithm(ABC):
    def __init__(self):
        self.name = "IOptimizationAlgorithm"
        self.xbest = None
        self.fbest = float('inf')
        self._eval_count = 0

    @property
    def Name(self) -> str:
        return self.name
    
    @Name.setter
    def Name(self, value: str):
        self.name = value

    @property
    def XBest(self) -> np.ndarray:
        return self.xbest
    
    @XBest.setter
    def XBest(self, value: np.ndarray):
        self.xbest = value

    @property
    def FBest(self) -> float:
        return self.fbest
    
    @FBest.setter
    def FBest(self, value: float):
        self.fbest = value

    @property
    def EvalCount(self) -> int:
        return self._eval_count
    
    @EvalCount.setter
    def EvalCount(self, value: int):
        self._eval_count = value

    @abstractmethod
    def Solve(self) -> float:
        pass
```

### 3. Implementacja klasy GWO

**Sygnatura konstruktora** (zgodna z BOA i PSO):
```python
class GWO(IOptimizationAlgorithm):
    def __init__(self, obj_func, dim: int, lb: np.ndarray, ub: np.ndarray,
                 pop_size: int, max_iter: int,
                 a_coeff: float = 2.0, c_coeff: float = 2.0):
```

**Parametry:**
- `obj_func`: funkcja celu do minimalizacji
- `dim`: liczba wymiarów problemu
- `lb`: dolne granice (lower bounds) jako np.ndarray
- `ub`: górne granice (upper bounds) jako np.ndarray
- `pop_size`: liczba wilków (search agents) - odpowiednik `wolves` z C#
- `max_iter`: maksymalna liczba iteracji
- `a_coeff`: współczynnik a (domyślnie 2.0, w C# było 1.0)
- `c_coeff`: współczynnik c (domyślnie 2.0, w C# było 3.0)

**Kluczowe elementy implementacji:**

1. **Inicjalizacja w `__init__`:**
   - Wywołać `super().__init__()`
   - Ustawić `self.Name = "Grey Wolf Optimizer"`
   - Zapisać wszystkie parametry jako atrybuty
   - Zainicjalizować `self.iteration_history = []` (odpowiednik `_iterationHistory`)

2. **Metoda `Solve()`:**
   - Zresetować `self.EvalCount = 0`
   - Zainicjalizować pozycje wilków losowo: `positions = np.random.uniform(lb, ub, (pop_size, dim))`
   - Obliczyć fitness dla każdej pozycji i zwiększyć `self.EvalCount`
   - Zainicjalizować alpha, beta, delta (3 najlepsze wilki):
     ```python
     alpha_score = float('inf')
     beta_score = float('inf')
     delta_score = float('inf')
     alpha_pos = np.zeros(dim)
     beta_pos = np.zeros(dim)
     delta_pos = np.zeros(dim)
     ```
   
3. **Główna pętla optymalizacji:**
   ```python
   for iter in range(max_iter):
       # Aktualizacja alpha, beta, delta
       for i in range(pop_size):
           fitness_i = obj_func(positions[i])
           self.EvalCount += 1
           
           if fitness_i < alpha_score:
               delta_score = beta_score
               delta_pos = beta_pos.copy()
               beta_score = alpha_score
               beta_pos = alpha_pos.copy()
               alpha_score = fitness_i
               alpha_pos = positions[i].copy()
           elif fitness_i < beta_score:
               delta_score = beta_score
               delta_pos = beta_pos.copy()
               beta_score = fitness_i
               beta_pos = positions[i].copy()
           elif fitness_i < delta_score:
               delta_score = fitness_i
               delta_pos = positions[i].copy()
       
       # Oblicz a (linearly decreases from a_coeff to 0)
       a = a_coeff - iter * (a_coeff / max_iter)
       
       # Aktualizacja pozycji wilków
       for i in range(pop_size):
           for j in range(dim):
               # Alpha
               r1 = np.random.random()
               r2 = np.random.random()
               A1 = 2.0 * a * r1 - a
               C1 = c_coeff * r2
               D_alpha = abs(C1 * alpha_pos[j] - positions[i, j])
               X1 = alpha_pos[j] - A1 * D_alpha
               
               # Beta
               r1 = np.random.random()
               r2 = np.random.random()
               A2 = 2.0 * a * r1 - a
               C2 = c_coeff * r2
               D_beta = abs(C2 * beta_pos[j] - positions[i, j])
               X2 = beta_pos[j] - A2 * D_beta
               
               # Delta
               r1 = np.random.random()
               r2 = np.random.random()
               A3 = 2.0 * a * r1 - a
               C3 = c_coeff * r2
               D_delta = abs(C3 * delta_pos[j] - positions[i, j])
               X3 = delta_pos[j] - A3 * D_delta
               
               # Średnia z trzech pozycji
               positions[i, j] = (X1 + X2 + X3) / 3.0
               
         ...

</details>

Example server structure:
```python
app = Server("resource-server")

@app.list_resources()
async def list_resources() -> list[Resource]:

items.title: Port Telos compiler from Python to C
items.body:
Full C implementation of the Telos compiler in `csrc/`, preserving all documented semantics: constraint-graph IR, polynomial closed-form detection, constant folding, x86-64 codegen, and C codegen.

### New files (`csrc/`)

- **`telos.h`** — All types: tokens, AST, IR, constraint graph, execution plans, public API
- **`lexer.c`** — Tokenizer (keywords, two-char operators, comments, float/int literals)
- **`parser.c`** — Recursive-descent parser, full expression precedence chain
- **`ir.c`** — IR node constructors, deep clone/free, algebraic simplifier, constant evaluator
- **`lifting.c`** — AST → constraint IR; accumulation loop → `ReductionConstraint` pattern detection
- **`optimizer.c`** — Polynomial decomposition, closed-form summation (degree ≤ 3), constant folding (≤10k iters), goal-graph liveness / dead-code elimination, cost-model plan selection
- **`codegen_x86.c`** — x86-64 machine code emission (System V AMD64 ABI, stack-slot allocation, jump patching)
- **`codegen_c.c`** — `long long`-based C source emission
- **`executable.c`** — `mmap(PROT_EXEC)` wrapper for native execution
- **`compiler.c`** — High-level API (`telos_compile`, `telos_run`, `telos_compile_c`) + CLI
- **`Makefile`** — `make` builds `telos`, `make test` runs suite
- **`tests/test_main.c`** — 45 tests across lexer, parser, lifting, optimizer, and end-to-end execution

### Verification

All optimizations from the spec are exercised end-to-end:

```bash

Covers: Gauss formula (`Σi`), sum-of-squares (`Σi²`), sum-of-cubes (`Σi³`), linear combinations, constant folding, inclusive ranges, multi-function files, identity/arithmetic functions.

items.title: Add Python/Cython mmuko_os package and replace C# compositor flow with Python/Cython
items.body:
### Motivation

- Provide a Python-native compositor that orchestrates the existing native NSIGII firmware while keeping the boot/runtime boundary stable on C/C++ artifacts.  
- Wrap existing native code (C sources and selected C++ exports) with Cython to offer a Python-friendly API that reproduces the public behavior previously exposed by the C# compositor.  
- Make the Python/Cython flow the primary build/run path for development and documentation.  
- Ensure headers and C++ exports are C-linkage friendly so the Cython extension can bind reliably without changing the native ABI.

### Description

- Adds a new package `python/mmuko_os` including `__main__.py`, `api.py`, and `HeartfullFirmware`/`HeartFeltFirmware` Python-facing API that mirrors `Main.cs` / `NSIGII_HeartfullFirmware.cs` behavior.  
- Implements Cython bindings in `python/mmuko_os/_firmware.pyx` and `.pxd` declaration files for `heartfull_firmware.h`, `bzy_mpda.h`, and `tripartite_discriminant.h`, plus `nsigii_cpp_wrapper` pxd to surface selected C++ helpers.  
- Adds packaging/build files (`setup.py`, `pyproject.toml`) and updates `Makefile` to make `make compositor` / `make run-compositor*` build and run the Python/Cython compositor instead of the legacy `.NET` path.  
- Makes headers C++-safe with `extern "C"` guards and adds `nsigii_cpp_wrapper.h` so the C++ wrapper exports are available to Cython, and updates `README.md` and `.gitignore` to document and ignore the new build artifacts.

### Testing

- Ran `make compositor` which builds the native libraries and installs the editable Python package successfully (build passed).  
- Executed the compositor CLI with `python3 -m mmuko_os --simulate-pass --tier1 yes --tier2 yes --w-actor yes` and it produced the expected PASS path and console output (succeeded).  
- Performed a Python smoke test using `PYTHONPATH=python` to exercise `HeartfullFirmware`/`HeartFeltFirmware` API and `populate_kanban`, verifying outcomes and rendered board (succeeded).  
- Ran `make verify` and exercised `make run-compositor-maybe` to validate HOLD/ALERT flows and observed the expected non-zero/alert behavior (succeeded).

items.title: Convert C++ inference to Python with ONNX Runtime
items.body:
Replaces ncnn-based C++ inference with Python implementation using ONNX Runtime. All original logic preserved.

## Implementation

- **`main.py`**: Port of `main.cpp` - handles video/image inference with ONNX Runtime
- **`tracking.py`**: Port of `tracking.cpp/h` - corner tracking via optical flow + grid-based feature extraction
- **`requirements.txt`**: numpy, opencv-python, onnxruntime

## Technical Notes

**Dimension handling**: ONNX model has swapped H/W dimensions
```python
# OpenCV: (240, 320, 3) → transpose to (1, 3, 320, 240) for ONNX
img_chw = np.transpose(img_rgb, (2, 0, 1))
img_chw_swapped = np.transpose(img_chw, (0, 2, 1))  # Swap H/W
```

**Preserved parameters**:
- Normalization: divide by 255
- Corner threshold: 0.2 (extracted as `CORNER_DETECTION_THRESHOLD`)
- Grid cell size: 20px
- Trajectory history: 5 frames (extracted as `MAX_TRAJECTORY_LENGTH`)

## Enhancements

- `--no-display` flag for headless execution
- `--output-dir` option for custom output paths
- Frame-by-frame PNG output

## Performance

~3-6ms per frame on CPU (comparable to C++ ncnn version at ~5ms)

items.title: 🔒 Fix command injection vulnerability in self-audit command
items.body:
🎯 **What:**
Fixed a potential code execution vulnerability in `src/telegram/bot.py` where `cmd_selfaudit` used `python -c` alongside an f-string to inject the project root path.

⚠️ **Risk:**
While `__file__` is generally predictable, constructing executable code dynamically via f-strings and executing it with `python -c` is a dangerous pattern. If any part of the path or injected variables contained specific characters (like single quotes or newlines), it could lead to arbitrary code execution or syntax errors, potentially compromising the host system.

🛡️ **Solution:**
Replaced the `python -c` execution with the safer module execution flag (`python -m src.core.self_editor`). To support this, I added the required `if __name__ == '__main__':` block to `src/core/self_editor.py` to handle logging initialization and execution of the improvement session directly. Added the learning to `.jules/sentinel.md`.

items.title: fix: replace sqlite-vec smoke test with load() call that catches ELFCLASS32
items.body:
## Summary

- The existing smoke test (`import sqlite_vec`) succeeds even when the `.so` bundled in the stable wheel is a 32-bit ARM ELF on an aarch64 system. The `ELFCLASS32` error only surfaces when `sqlite_vec.load()` is actually called against a SQLite connection.
- This means the 3-stage fallback in `install.sh` (stable → alpha 0.1.7a2 → source build) never triggered, leaving the system with a broken sqlite-vec that silently disabled vector search.
- Replace all three smoke test invocations with a one-liner that opens an in-memory DB and calls `sqlite_vec.load()`, so the fallback chain fires correctly.

## Changes

All three smoke tests in `install.sh` (lines ~1254, ~1266, ~1282) changed from:
```bash
python -c "import sqlite_vec"
```
to:
```bash
python -c "import sqlite3, sqlite_vec; c=sqlite3.connect(':memory:'); c.enable_load_extension(True); sqlite_vec.load(c)"
```

## Test plan

- [ ] On an aarch64 system with sqlite-vec 0.1.6 installed, the stable-release check now fails and the fallback to 0.1.7a2 (or source build) triggers
- [ ] On a working x86_64 system, the stable-release check still passes on the first attempt

items.title: Fixes for C++ to Python future notifications
items.body:
Fix missing reference counting for asyncio event loop in Python futures, `_asyncioEventLoop` pointer in `ucxx::python::Future` was stored without incrementing its reference count, creating a potential use-after-free bug if the Python garbage collector deallocates the event loop while the `Future` object still holds a pointer to it.

Added checks in `future_set_result_with_event_loop()` and `future_set_exception_with_event_loop()` to validate that the event loop pointer is not `NULL` and points to a valid Python object before attempting to call methods on it. During shutdown or in race conditions, the event loop may be deallocated while futures are still being processed. Without these checks, accessing a deallocated event loop can cause segmentation faults.

items.title: [grove_tb6612fng] Move direction logic from Python to C++ to fix lambda crash
items.body:
# What does this implement/fix?

Fix `TypeError` crash when using a lambda for `speed` with `direction: BACKWARD` in the `grove_tb6612fng.run` action.

The Python codegen negated the speed with `-template_speed` to implement backward direction. When speed is a lambda, `cg.templatable()` returns a `LambdaExpression` object which has no `__neg__` operator, crashing codegen.

**Fix:** Move the direction logic into the C++ `GROVETB6612FNGMotorRunAction::play()` method:
- Add `set_direction(bool)` setter and `forward_` field
- Negate speed in `play()` when direction is backward  
- Python passes direction as a bool instead of negating speed

This also fixes a type mismatch: speed was stored as `uint16_t` but `dc_motor_run()` takes `int16_t`. The cast now happens explicitly in `play()` where the negation is applied.

items.title: [Python] Start switching ROOT pythonizations to Python Limited C API
items.body:
This patch replaces usage of CPython macros and non-stable APIs with functions compatible with the Python Limited C API (stable ABI).

Replace direct macros with API functions:

  * `PyBytes_AS_STRING`   : `PyBytes_AsString`
  * `PyBytes_GET_SIZE`    : `PyBytes_Size`
  * `PyTuple_GET_SIZE`    : `PyTuple_Size`
  * `PyTuple_SET_ITEM`    : `PyTuple_SetItem`
  * `PyList_GET_ITEM`     : `PyList_GetItem`
  * `PyList_GET_SIZE`     : `PyList_Size`
  * `PyInt_AS_LONG`       : `PyInt_AsLong`
  * `Py_DECREF`           : `Py_DecRef`
  * `Py_XDECREF`          : `Py_DecRef`

Furthermore, replace `PyUnicode_AsUTF8` with a helper based on the stable `PyUnicode_AsUTF8AndSize`. Also, replace `<Python.h>` with `"PythonLimitedAPI.h"`, where the `Py_LIMITED_API` macro is set.

This change is part of enabling PyROOT to build against the Python stable ABI.

items.title: Multi-language LSP platform: Rust, Go, Python, C++ with capability negotiation, workspace detection, and UI notifications
items.body:
Expands the LSP subsystem from C++/Python-only to a full four-language platform (Rust, Go, Python, C++) with proper capability negotiation, project root detection, per-language settings, server health tracking, and user-facing UI notifications for server status and errors.

### Server configs
- Default configs for `rust-analyzer`, `gopls`, `pylsp`, `clangd --background-index`
- Per-language settings override via `languageServers.{lang}.{command,arguments,environment,enabled}` in SettingsManager

### LSP client extensions
- New requests: `requestFormatting`, `requestDeclaration`, `requestTypeDefinition`, `requestWorkspaceSymbols`
- `LspServerCapabilities` struct parsed from `initialize` response — graceful fallback when server lacks a feature
- `supportsCapability(name)` via member-pointer map for clean lookup:
```cpp
static const QMap<QString, bool LspServerCapabilities::*> capMap = {
    {"hover", &LspServerCapabilities::hoverProvider},
    {"formatting", &LspServerCapabilities::documentFormattingProvider},
    // ...
};
```

### Workspace detection
- `detectProjectRoot()` walks up from file looking for `Cargo.toml`, `go.mod`, `pyproject.toml`, `requirements.txt`, `setup.py`, `CMakeLists.txt`, `compile_commands.json`
- Root URI auto-set on client before `didOpen`

### Server health
- `ServerHealthStatus` enum (Unknown/Starting/Running/Error/Stopped) tracked per language
- `serverHealthChanged` signal for UI status indicators

### UI notifications and status feedback
- **Toast notifications** (`NotificationWidget`) — modern non-blocking popups in the bottom-right with fade animation, color-coded accent bars, and auto-dismiss. Error toasts for server failures (e.g. "Failed to start 'clangd'. Is it installed?"), warning toasts for misconfiguration, and info toasts when a server becomes ready.
- **Status bar indicator** — color-coded label in the bottom bar showing language server health per language: green `●` running, yellow `◌` starting, red `✖` error, gray `○` disabled, with descriptive tooltips.
- `serverError`, `serverStarted`, and `serverHealthChanged` signals wired to both toast notifications and status bar updates.

### Tests
- Extended existing tests with Rust/Go assertions
- New cases: project root detection for each language ecosystem, health tracking, capability defaults, enabled field

### Docs
- `docs/LANGUAGE_SERVERS.md`: installation, configuration, workspace detection, troubleshooting

items.title: Implement C++ search engine with Stockfish-inspired optimizations
items.body:
Moves core minimax search from Python to C++ while preserving Python-based game control. Achieves 100-968x speedup using Stockfish techniques.

## Implementation

**C++ Search Engine** (`search_engine.cpp`)
- Minimax with alpha-beta pruning
- Transposition table with configurable size (1M entries default)
- Move ordering heuristics (value priority + center control)
- Optimized evaluation eliminating redundant move generation

**Python Integration** (pybind11)
- High-level game logic, UI, and configuration remain in Python
- AI delegates search to C++ when available, falls back to pure Python
- Zero API changes to existing code

```python
# Automatic C++ usage - no code changes required
ai = SequenciumAI(max_depth=5)
move = ai.get_best_move(board, Player.A)
# Uses C++ engine: 0.2ms, 358 nodes

# Force Python implementation
ai = SequenciumAI(max_depth=5, use_cpp=False)
move = ai.get_best_move(board, Player.A)  
# Pure Python: 190ms, 2298 nodes
```

## Performance

| Depth | Python | C++ | Speedup | Node Reduction |
|-------|--------|-----|---------|----------------|
| 3 | 8ms, 109 nodes | 0.08ms, 48 nodes | 100x | 56% |
| 4 | 32ms, 414 nodes | 0.11ms, 106 nodes | 291x | 74% |
| 5 | 190ms, 2298 nodes | 0.22ms, 358 nodes | 864x | 84% |

## Build

```bash
pip install pybind11
python3 setup.py build_ext --inplace
```

Compiler flags: `-O3 -march=native -ffast-math` (see BUILD.md for portability)

items.title: Move function call path from Python to C++ for cached calls
items.body:
- Add m_cache member to NativeFunctionNode with set_cache/resolve_cache
- Implement full_call() in C++ handling _result, _append_to, and error formatting
- Register full_call as __call__ via nanobind, eliminating Python frames on hot path
- Set cache pointer in Function.__init__, refresh on hot reload
- Remove Python __call__, simplify call() to delegate to C++ __call__
- Clean up dead code: _handle_error, _cache_value_to_id, unused imports

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **New Features**
  * Functions are callable from Python using normal call syntax with a writable native cache pointer accessible for advanced use.

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Runtime call errors now include richer, reformatted messages with additional context to aid debugging.

* **Refactor**
  * Call and append behavior and cache lifecycle reworked to improve call reliability and ensure hot-reloads propagate to existing function wrappers.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->


## Before/After Timing

| Benchmark | Before (main) | After (this PR) | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| slangpy_manual_hook | 0.158ms | 0.153ms | **+3.2%** |
| slangpy_automatic | 0.167ms | 0.164ms | **+1.8%** |

Measured with `pytest slangpy/benchmarks/test_benchmark_autograd.py` on CUDA, 1000 iterations after 10 warmups.

The improvement comes from eliminating Python frames on the cached call path — `__call__` now goes directly to C++ instead of through Python `call()` → `_native_call()`.

items.title: chore(docker): trim production image — 17% size reduction
items.body:

- Strip **pip**, **setuptools**, and their metadata from the installed prefix in the builder stage so they never land in shipped layers
- Remove `RECORD`, `SOURCES.txt`, `direct_url.json` from `.dist-info` directories

items.title: Pure-Python refactor: replace C++ service with app pipeline, add downloader/transcriber/translator and tests
items.body:
### Motivation

- Move from a hybrid C++/Python monolith to a self-contained, pure-Python media pipeline to simplify deployment and development.
- Remove legacy C++ service, Docker compose and CI workflow artifacts that are not needed for the new Python-first flow.
- Provide a reproducible, easier-to-test pipeline for download, ASR, translation and final compose using `faster-whisper`, `yt-dlp` and `ffmpeg`.

### Description

- Introduce a new Python package `app` with modules: `settings.py`, `downloader.py`, `ffmpeg_tools.py`, `transcriber.py`, `translator.py`, `subtitles.py`, and `pipeline.py`, plus an `__init__.py` placeholder. 
- Replace environment template `.env.example` to expose the new runtime keys such as `WORK_DIR`, `OUTPUT_NAME`, `WHISPER_*`, `YTDLP_PROXY`, `TARGET_LANGUAGE` and remove C++/server-specific variables. 
- Remove legacy C++ server sources, Dockerfiles, docker-compose and old scripts and CI workflow files, and update `README.md` and `pyproject.toml` to describe the pure-Python flow and new CLI entry `yp-run`. 
- Add unit/integration style tests under `tests/` and a helper `tests/run_stage_checks.py` to run staged checks, covering downloader, transcriber, translator, subtitles, ffmpeg helper and an e2e pipeline run with mocks. 

### Testing

- Ran the test suite with `python -m unittest discover -s tests -v`, which executed staged unit tests for `tests.test_downloader_stage`, `tests.test_transcriber_stage`, `tests.test_translator_stage`, `tests.test_subtitles_stage`, `tests.test_ffmpeg_stage`, and `tests.test_pipeline_e2e`. All tests passed. 
- Added `tests/run_stage_checks.py` as a convenience script to run the same staged checks via `python tests/run_stage_checks.py`, which also completed successfully in CI-local validation.

items.title: ⚡ Bolt: implement arena allocator pattern to replace self-referential structs
items.body:
This PR adds a comprehensive implementation of the **Self-Referential Data Structures (Arena Allocator / Indices)** pattern, addressing a core paradigm shift needed when moving from Java/Python/C++ to Rust. It demonstrates how to effectively build graphs and complex structures using contiguous memory and stable indices, thereby sidestepping borrow checker errors and avoiding memory leaks associated with `Rc<RefCell<T>>` soups.

Additionally, this PR fixes a pre-existing failing test in `src/concurrency/thread_local.rs` which was blocking the `cargo test` suite from succeeding.
